column2: — Marta, what screws?
He won't do anything.
— And who will?
— The one who did our roof, he'd do it… but this one?
At the market they went where they sell nails and hammers by weight, and began looking for screws, not really knowing what they even looked like.
They walked between the rows, where iron bits of every possible size lay on the ground, and over them, like broody hens, the men clucked with their shirts unbuttoned, and some with no shirts at all.
The rows flickered with chests bearing white, black, gray, ginger hair and completely bald ones, and on the chests dozens of pairs of nipples abounded — from pale to dark brown.
The men clucked, sweated, shouted.
There were ads for carpenters, layers and tilers.
Marta was beginning to feel relentlessly nauseous.
Her head spun, her bright skirt began to sink toward the ground.
She sat down under a sign about discounts on fur, on which a naked tanned woman in a fur coat was depicted.
— Marta!
Marta!
— Miyechka tried to catch her under the arms, but, being almost half her size, couldn't prevent Marta from sitting down on the ground.
From behind the bearings two men leapt out, one — in an unbuttoned shirt, and the other — naked to the waist.
And they dragged Marta by the arms into a shop.
There she came to, trying not to look at the men who flashed their bare chests before her eyes and smelled of sweat mixed with cologne.
Those men left.
— How are you, Marta?
— asked Miyechka.
— Fine, my head spun.
Beside them stood an older man in a white short-sleeved shirt tucked into his trousers.
He was clean-shaven, smelling unobtrusively of some light perfume.
After the row of bearings he seemed like the Lord God.
Which wasn't far from the truth.
The girls looked around.
Light barely made its way into the shop — there was one window and that was covered with blinds.
On the counters — neatly laid out tools.
— I'm Kyrylo, — said the man and held out his hand to Miyechka.
Behind his back light came through the window, and it seemed that his head was glowing.
— Solomiya.
Marta sat on a chair, her black locks still lying straight.
She didn't allow herself to get disheveled, even when she fainted.
Kyrylo smiled, stood straight.
His face was angelically bright.
The shop was run by Seventh-day Adventists, or Jehovah's Witnesses, or just Baptists.
They didn't drink, didn't curse, didn't dance.
They worked and washed.
— And the main thing, — Marta said, already driving back in the car and holding Kyrylo's business card, — the main thing is that they wear shirts.
They didn't buy any screws.
The thin man with the inconspicuous ginger whiskers, who had come to do the laying, sat for an hour, folded in half.
Then fell asleep.
Then woke up, got on his bicycle and rode off, saying he'd come 'day after tomorrow.'
And Kyrylo came along with twelve apostles the very next day, as soon as Marta had called him the evening before.
He said he'd come at nine in the morning, and came at nine.
After Kyrylo, one after another, like angels, his craftsmen got out of the minibus.
— They're here?
— the grandmother sprang to her feet.
In all her ninety-six years she had never yet seen the local drunkard-handymen arrive at the appointed hour.
Kyrylo walked first and smiled.
All the women came out onto the old terrace.
Miyechka was still in her pajamas.
Marta in the same skirt and the grandfather's shirt.
Lilichka peeked out of the door still in her underwear, holding the naked Lusya in her arms.
Only Maria and the grandmother, as befits the eldest in the family, were dressed up properly.
Maria stepped forward and held out her hand to Kyrylo.
— Maria.
— Kyrylo, — he beamed with a smile, his white T-shirt shone in the sun.
Kyrylo didn't smell of alcohol, fuel oil and hopelessness.
Behind him came the craftsmen, carrying their tool cases with them.
All twelve apostles laid the boards skillfully.
They sawed, clicked, tapped, hammered, drove in nails and sanded.
The terrace rose from the ashes, like some biblical miracle.
— It's simply something incredible, — the grandmother whispered into Miyechka's ear, as she sat beside her and also couldn't tear her eyes away from the miracle of the terrace's rising.
— Girls, — the grandmother said now to Miyechka and Lilichka, who had after all put on shorts and come out to sit beside them.
— I feel downright awkward that we just sit here and watch, but it's impossible to look away.
When the drill stopped working and the sanding began, Kyrylo struck up a song.
The twelve apostles picked it up.
They sang about love, about Christ, about the high mountains and forgiveness.
— How beautifully they sing, — said the grandmother.
Marta too came out now to the singing.
She felt better.
Behind her appeared Maria with Lusya.
— Heavens, heavens, sun-radiant love, — sang Kyrylo with the apostles.
— Girls, — the grandmother whispered again theatrically.
— Make them some lemonade.
The sun was beating down, but not one of the apostles, including Kyrylo, even thought of taking off his shirt.
— Look, they don't undress, — said Marta.
— We'll never find out which of them is hairy.
— I doubt they're of flesh and blood, — said Miyechka.
— O pure angel, washed by the heavens, — he began to sing.
All the apostles seemed to have perfect tenors, just the thing for conquering women's hearts.
Lilichka went to the kitchen and began to mix lemonade, unable to keep herself from glancing out the window.
From the terrace a song rang out.
Miyechka came for the glasses and carried them six in each hand.
— You'd break all the dishes, Miyechka, just to avoid making two trips, — said the grandmother.
Miyechka heard this phrase almost every day, so she even lifted one leg, as if about to do an arabesque, to tease the grandmother a little more.
— And nothing will be left for your dowry, not a single glass, — said the grandmother.
She repeated this phrase all the time too, even when everyone had already bought themselves three dozen identical white plates from IKEA for their dowry.
Miyechka and Lilichka dragged over another bench.

When the song finally died down and the terrace rose from the ruins like a marvelous creation of the Lord, Miyechka, having changed from her pajamas into a light sky-blue dress, the color of which strangely suited the apostles, came up to the terrace and said:
— Come and have some lemonade.
A very young lad with dark-ginger hair and a face pale as snow without a single freckle raised his eyes to her.
The lad smiled and said quietly:
— We still need to sweep up and clean everything.
Miyechka nodded and bounded off to the table where the grandmother sat.
— Granny, — Miyechka cried out, and then came up to her very close, tucked her locks behind her ear and leaned right down to the grandmother's face.
— You're going to fall over now.
— Tell me, — she answered.
Marta, Lilichka and Maria with Lusya also listened in.
— They're going to clean up now, — Miyechka pronounced syllable by syllable.
— Maria!
— the grandmother tossed out.
— Maria, go and feel one of them, to see whether they're even people at all.

And while Kyrylo and the apostles cleaned the terrace, resuming the songs that rang out over the house, over the yard, over the ivy, the bushes and the raspberry, the wicket gate opened and a man came in, wheeling a bicycle.
It was that thin, wretched drunkard-handyman, and he looked worse than the day before.
He stood by the gate, swaying, and, squinting, looked at the terrace that had risen from yesterday's rotted boards.

Not one of the girls even had time to come up to the drunkard-handyman to explain to him in terms he could grasp that the order had 'got fucked up,' before one of the apostles, who along with four others was on his knees picking shavings out of the grass under the terrace, jumped up to the drunkard-handyman and said:
— Brother!
Come and have some lemonade.
He wasn't heard to answer anything, but he leaned the bicycle against the wall and came up to the bench that was already prepared for the apostles.
Perhaps he had heard the key word 'have a drink,' and what came after meant nothing to him anymore.
Having cleaned the terrace to a shine and neatly gathered the shavings and sawdust into a large black bag, the apostles sat down on the bench.
Kyrylo, like Jesus Christ at the Last Supper, sat in the center.
Marta gave each one a glass, in which, amid the mint and lemons of the lemonade, clear pieces of ice floated.
The drunkard-handyman also took a glass and waited for a toast.
There was no toast.
Everyone began sipping just like that, and he tried too, and then, disappointed, stared at the sky for a very long time.
The drunkard-handyman's name was Valeriy.
In the sun you could confirm that under his nose there were indeed whiskers.
— Valeriy, are you happy in life?
— Kyrylo asked him with a broad smile.
Valeriy gave an uncertain sort of twitch.
— Have you come to know love, Valeriy?
— Kyrylo asked again, raising his eyebrows good-naturedly.
His gaze was pure as the heavens.
Valeriy held in his hand a barely sipped glass, glancing at it as if it were to blame for there being lemonade in it.

He looked at it without obvious condemnation, the way parents look at a child who has brought home a failing grade from school — with vexation, slight disappointment, but also with hope that it would suddenly mend its ways and stop being a glass of lemonade.
And in the company of the apostles one particularly wanted to believe in miracles.
— So have you come to know love, Valeriy?
— Kyrylo repeated.
Valeriy looked at him with vexation and asked:
— Do you mean about women..?
And while Marta, who stood closest, snorted, and the grandmother turned away so her reaction wouldn't be seen, not a single muscle moved on the faces of all twelve apostles and their leader Kyrylo.
— It's horrible!
— Lilichka would say afterward.
— They're like robots!

Kyrylo struck up a song about love, which began like a classic love serenade, but after the refrain it became clear that there would be no ardent kisses by the river, because it was about love for Christ.
Lusya yawned.
Maria took her in her arms and began to rock her gently.
The little girl took hold of Maria's ear.
Another song began.
This time it all began as though it were about horses and adventures.
But it ended, by some twist of the plot, with the Lord God.
Valeriy drank the lemonade down in one gulp, screwing his eyes shut, and tried to get up.
Kyrylo firmly put his arm around his shoulder, without stopping the song — this time they were singing about the whole planet.
Lusya fell asleep.
The grandmother beckoned Lilichka over and asked:
— How much longer can this go on?
The songs did not let up.
— Maybe more lemonade?
— Miyechka managed to squeeze in during a pause.
But Kyrylo just shook his head, and the twelve apostles caught his reaction and struck up a new one.
Marta sprang to her feet and, snatching Lusya out of Maria's arms, despite Maria's 'you mustn't lift anything heavy, where are you carrying her,' hurried off to the house at a run.
Maria got up and said:
— I'll help put the little one to bed, — and ran after Marta.
They shut the windows and the front door in the house, but the sweet tenors rang out so that there was no way to hide from them.
Even in the farthest room, which faced the raspberry that seemed to muffle not only sounds but to fence one off from the outside world altogether, the songs could be heard.
— Maybe they'd be better off just getting drunk, — Marta whispered furiously, laying Lusya on the sofa, who now wouldn't let go of Marta's ear.
Outside, meanwhile, four hostages of the divine songs remained — Miyechka, Lilichka, the grandmother and the drunkard-handyman Valeriy.
— Granny, Maria and Marta used the child, so you say that you're old and it's time for you to rest, — Lilichka whispered.
— Me, old?
— said the grandmother.
This joke hadn't lost its relevance for twenty years.
The apostles, led by Kyrylo, rose from the bench half an hour later.
Miyechka's head was buzzing.
They led Valeriy by the arms, and he, as if sobered up, jumped on his bicycle and rode off briskly, without his characteristic sluggishness.
As if he'd been healed.
— I once had a suitor, — the grandmother began, as soon as the minibus with the apostles had rolled down the hill onto the road.
— He was as good as all these twelve apostles put together.
When something needed doing, he'd help in such a way that it was as if he were the one in your debt.
— And why did you brush him off, then?
— asked Miyechka, gathering the glasses from the table.
— I was a fool.
— And honestly?
— He made me sick, Solomiya.
The new terrace had to be painted, and for half a day all the women argued about the color.
The grandmother was for the color of rotten cherry.
Lilichka retorted that the very name made her queasy and that the former terrace had been a rotten color, while this one should be different.
Marta said any color would do, so long as the paint didn't stink too much.
Maria wanted blue, the way they paint doors on the Greek islands.
And Miyechka said that blue without the sea and white stones would look like all the verandas of the eighties.
In the end they settled on Provençal — a barely noticeable lavender mixed with gray.
— It'll look as if it's already aged and merged with its surroundings, — Miyechka argued.
— And as if it's already peeling, — the grandmother added.
— It's so it doesn't catch the eye, — Miyechka insisted.
— To make it look already aged you probably have to pay a few hundred extra on top, — the grandmother was always the most stubborn.

She pretended she agreed with everything, but everyone already knew now that for as long as the terrace stood, or, to put it more aptly, for as long as the grandmother lived, she would keep talking about the peeling Provençal chic.
And for that alone it was already worth painting the terrace in aged Provençal.
